prompt: What happens to a PaaS service's public IP when you configure a service endpoint for it?
choices: It keeps its public IP but can be restricted to the selected VNet subnet(s)
The public IP is removed and replaced by a private IP
It receives an additional private IP alongside the public one
It becomes reachable only through a VPN Gateway
correctAnswers: It keeps its public IP but can be restricted to the selected VNet subnet(s)
explanation: A service endpoint extends your VNet identity over the backbone; the service keeps its public IP but access can be limited to allowed subnets.

prompt: What does a private endpoint give a PaaS service?
choices: A private IP inside your VNet via a dedicated NIC, with no public exposure
A faster, dedicated public IP
A restriction of its public IP to your subnet
An encrypted tunnel from the service to on-premises
correctAnswers: A private IP inside your VNet via a dedicated NIC, with no public exposure
explanation: A private endpoint (Azure Private Link) assigns the service a private IP on a NIC in your VNet. Restricting a public IP by subnet is what a service endpoint does.

prompt: Which requirement is unique to private endpoints (and not to service endpoints) for name resolution to work correctly?
choices: Integrating a Private DNS zone so the service name resolves to the private IP
Adding the Storage service tag to the subnet NSG
Enabling a VPN Gateway on the VNet
Assigning a managed identity to the client
correctAnswers: Integrating a Private DNS zone so the service name resolves to the private IP
explanation: Without Private DNS zone integration, the service's hostname still resolves to its public IP, bypassing the private endpoint.

prompt: A PaaS service must have NO public exposure and be reachable from peered VNets and on-premises over ExpressRoute. Which option meets this?
choices: A private endpoint with public network access disabled
A service endpoint restricting the public IP to your subnet
An NSG rule allowing only the on-premises IP range
A shared access signature scoped to the service
correctAnswers: A private endpoint with public network access disabled
explanation: Only a private endpoint removes public exposure while remaining reachable across peered VNets and on-premises links. Service endpoints keep a public IP.

prompt: After adding a private endpoint to a storage account, what additional setting ensures it can no longer be reached over the public internet?
choices: Set Public network access to Disabled
Enable soft delete
Rotate the account access keys
Enable blob versioning
correctAnswers: Set Public network access to Disabled
explanation: Adding a private endpoint does not automatically close the public endpoint; you must disable public network access explicitly.

prompt: What is an Azure Private Link service (as opposed to a private endpoint)?
choices: A way to expose YOUR own service (behind a Standard Load Balancer) privately to consumers
A way to consume a Microsoft PaaS service privately
A managed DNS resolver for private endpoints
A global CDN entry point
correctAnswers: A way to expose YOUR own service (behind a Standard Load Balancer) privately to consumers
explanation: A Private Link service publishes your own service for others to reach via their own private endpoints — the mirror image of consuming a service privately.

prompt: What does VNet integration enable for an Azure App Service or Function app?
choices: Outbound calls from the app into a VNet
Inbound calls into the app restricted to a VNet
Encryption of the app's data at rest
A private IP for the app's public endpoint
correctAnswers: Outbound calls from the app into a VNet
explanation: VNet integration handles outbound connectivity into a VNet; securing inbound access requires a private endpoint or access restrictions.

prompt: An App Service must call an on-premises SQL Server over a site-to-site VPN, while keeping its normal public inbound endpoint. What should you configure?
choices: VNet integration on the App Service, connected to the VPN-linked VNet
A private endpoint on the App Service
An App Service Environment
A service endpoint on the App Service
correctAnswers: VNet integration on the App Service, connected to the VPN-linked VNet
explanation: VNet integration is the outbound-only mechanism, letting the app reach the on-premises SQL Server without altering its public inbound endpoint.

prompt: Which App Service networking feature secures INBOUND access to the app?
choices: A private endpoint (or access restrictions)
VNet integration
A hybrid connection
A service endpoint on the outbound subnet
correctAnswers: A private endpoint (or access restrictions)
explanation: Inbound protection for App Service comes from a private endpoint or access restrictions; VNet integration only handles outbound traffic.

prompt: What is an App Service Environment (ASE)?
choices: A fully isolated, single-tenant App Service deployment inside your VNet
A shared multi-tenant App Service plan with a private endpoint attached
A staging slot for App Service deployments
A managed DNS zone for App Service apps
correctAnswers: A fully isolated, single-tenant App Service deployment inside your VNet
explanation: An ASE is the single-tenant, fully isolated App Service hosting option, at higher cost/management than multi-tenant App Service.

prompt: When is an ASE the right choice over multi-tenant App Service with VNet integration and a private endpoint?
choices: When a hard requirement mandates full single-tenant isolation
When you want the lowest cost and least management
When the app is a simple public website with no isolation needs
When you only need outbound access to a VNet
correctAnswers: When a hard requirement mandates full single-tenant isolation
explanation: ASE's justification is strict single-tenant isolation. For lower cost/overhead or simple needs, multi-tenant App Service with networking features is preferable.

prompt: How is Azure SQL Managed Instance deployed at the network level?
choices: Always into a delegated subnet you secure with NSGs and route tables
Always with a public endpoint and no network controls
Only inside an App Service Environment
Only reachable through Azure Bastion
correctAnswers: Always into a delegated subnet you secure with NSGs and route tables
explanation: SQL Managed Instance always lands in a delegated subnet you control; you can also add a private endpoint for cross-VNet/on-prem access.

prompt: Between service endpoints and private endpoints, which keeps the service's public IP address?
choices: Service endpoint
Private endpoint
Both remove the public IP
Neither uses IP addressing
correctAnswers: Service endpoint
explanation: Service endpoints restrict a still-public IP to your subnet; private endpoints assign a private IP and remove public exposure.

prompt: A team enabled a service endpoint for Storage but the account is still reachable from other Azure tenants' subnets. Why?
choices: A service endpoint alone doesn't restrict access — you must also set the storage firewall to allow only your subnet
Service endpoints only work with private DNS zones
The account needs a private endpoint before any restriction applies
Service endpoints require public network access to be disabled first
correctAnswers: A service endpoint alone doesn't restrict access — you must also set the storage firewall to allow only your subnet
explanation: The service endpoint marks traffic with your subnet identity, but you still configure the storage account firewall to allow only that subnet and deny others.

prompt: Which sub-resource would you select when creating a private endpoint for a storage account that serves blob data?
choices: blob
vault
sqlServer
registry
correctAnswers: blob
explanation: Private endpoints are created per sub-resource (target group); for blob data you select the 'blob' sub-resource. Others belong to Key Vault, SQL, or ACR.

prompt: From on-premises over ExpressRoute private peering, a client resolves a storage account name to its PUBLIC IP even though a private endpoint exists. What is the fix?
choices: Configure DNS forwarding/conditional forwarding to the Azure Private DNS zone
Add the storage service tag to the on-premises firewall
Recreate the private endpoint with a public IP
Enable a service endpoint in addition to the private endpoint
correctAnswers: Configure DNS forwarding/conditional forwarding to the Azure Private DNS zone
explanation: On-premises resolvers must be pointed at (or forward to) the Private DNS zone so the name resolves to the private endpoint IP rather than the public one.

prompt: Which statement about a Private Link service versus a private endpoint is correct?
choices: A Private Link service is what a provider publishes; a private endpoint is what a consumer creates to reach it
They are two names for the same object
A Private Link service consumes a Microsoft PaaS service privately
A private endpoint requires a Standard Load Balancer on the consumer side
correctAnswers: A Private Link service is what a provider publishes; a private endpoint is what a consumer creates to reach it
explanation: The provider stands up a Private Link service (behind a Standard LB); each consumer connects with a private endpoint on their side.

prompt: Which two networking directions do VNet integration and a private endpoint respectively cover for App Service?
choices: VNet integration = outbound; private endpoint = inbound
VNet integration = inbound; private endpoint = outbound
Both cover outbound only
Both cover inbound only
correctAnswers: VNet integration = outbound; private endpoint = inbound
explanation: They solve opposite directions and are frequently used together.

prompt: You want a Key Vault to be reachable only from within a specific VNet, with no public endpoint at all. Which is the most robust option?
choices: A private endpoint on the Key Vault with public network access disabled
A service endpoint plus the vault firewall set to the subnet
An NSG rule on the vault's subnet
A Conditional Access policy on the vault
correctAnswers: A private endpoint on the Key Vault with public network access disabled
explanation: A private endpoint removes public exposure entirely; a service endpoint still leaves the vault's public endpoint reachable (restricted). NSGs/Conditional Access don't gate the vault's public endpoint this way.

prompt: Which is a genuine advantage of a private endpoint over a service endpoint?
choices: It works across peered VNets and on-premises networks, with no public exposure
It is free, whereas service endpoints are billed per hour
It requires no DNS configuration
It automatically encrypts data at rest in the service
correctAnswers: It works across peered VNets and on-premises networks, with no public exposure
explanation: Private endpoints extend privately across peered/on-prem networks and remove public exposure. They do require Private DNS configuration and are not free.

prompt: A serverless Function app needs to reach a database that only accepts traffic from within a VNet. Which feature should you enable on the Function app?
choices: VNet integration
A private endpoint on the Function app
A service endpoint on the database only
Azure Bastion
correctAnswers: VNet integration
explanation: The Function app makes an outbound call into the VNet, which is VNet integration's role. A private endpoint on the app would address inbound access instead.

prompt: Which of these is NOT a valid way to reduce public exposure of an Azure SQL Database (logical server)?
choices: Assigning it a system-assigned managed identity
Adding a private endpoint
Disabling public network access
Restricting the server firewall to specific IPs/VNets
correctAnswers: Assigning it a system-assigned managed identity
explanation: A managed identity is about how the server authenticates outbound (e.g. to Key Vault), not about restricting inbound network exposure. The other three all reduce public reachability.

prompt: Why is Azure SQL Managed Instance considered network-isolated by design?
choices: It is always deployed into a delegated subnet that you control with NSGs and routes
It is always assigned a private endpoint automatically
It can only be reached through Azure Bastion
It runs inside a mandatory App Service Environment
correctAnswers: It is always deployed into a delegated subnet that you control with NSGs and routes
explanation: SQL MI is never placed in a shared multi-tenant network; it always lives in a delegated subnet you secure directly.

prompt: A private endpoint is created but clients inside the VNet still connect to the service's public IP. What is the most likely cause?
choices: The Private DNS zone was not linked to the VNet, so the name still resolves publicly
The private endpoint needs a public IP assigned
Public network access must be re-enabled
The subnet is missing a service endpoint
correctAnswers: The Private DNS zone was not linked to the VNet, so the name still resolves publicly
explanation: Without the Private DNS zone linked to the VNet, clients resolve the public IP and never use the private endpoint.

prompt: Which two options keep traffic to a PaaS service off the public internet? (Choose two.)
choices: Service endpoints
Private endpoints
Allowing 0.0.0.0/0 on the NSG
Disabling TLS on the service
correctAnswers: Service endpoints
Private endpoints
explanation: Both service and private endpoints keep PaaS traffic on the Azure backbone/off the public internet, at different depths.

prompt: Which two statements about service endpoints are correct? (Choose two.)
choices: The PaaS service keeps a public IP address
Traffic travels over the Azure backbone
The service is assigned a private IP inside the VNet
A Private DNS zone is mandatory for them to function
correctAnswers: The PaaS service keeps a public IP address
Traffic travels over the Azure backbone
explanation: Service endpoints keep the public IP (restricted by subnet) and route over the backbone; private IPs and Private DNS are private-endpoint concepts.

prompt: Which two reasons justify an App Service Environment over multi-tenant App Service? (Choose two.)
choices: A regulatory requirement for full single-tenant isolation
A need for a dedicated, isolated deployment
A desire to minimize cost and management overhead
A simple public marketing site with no isolation need
correctAnswers: A regulatory requirement for full single-tenant isolation
A need for a dedicated, isolated deployment
explanation: ASE is justified by strict isolation needs, not by cost minimization or simple public sites.

prompt: Referring to requirement 3 (a backups storage account reachable only from an on-premises network over ExpressRoute, zero public exposure), which configuration is correct?
choices: A private endpoint on the storage account with public network access disabled, reached over ExpressRoute private peering
A service endpoint restricting the public IP to the on-premises range
A SAS token issued to the on-premises app
The storage firewall allowing the on-premises public IP
correctAnswers: A private endpoint on the storage account with public network access disabled, reached over ExpressRoute private peering
explanation: Only a private endpoint with public access disabled removes all public exposure while remaining reachable from an ExpressRoute-connected network.

prompt: Referring to requirement 4 (App Service must make outbound calls to on-premises SQL over a site-to-site VPN, keeping its public inbound endpoint), what should be configured?
choices: VNet integration connected to the VPN-linked VNet, leaving the public inbound endpoint unchanged
A private endpoint on the App Service
An App Service Environment
A service endpoint for SQL on the App Service
correctAnswers: VNet integration connected to the VPN-linked VNet, leaving the public inbound endpoint unchanged
explanation: VNet integration provides outbound reach into the VPN-connected network without touching the app's public inbound endpoint.

prompt: After deploying the private endpoint for the backups storage account, on-premises clients still resolve its public name. What must Fabrikam fix?
choices: Point on-premises DNS (conditional forwarding) at the Azure Private DNS zone for the storage endpoint
Disable the private endpoint and use a service endpoint instead
Add the Storage service tag to the on-premises firewall
Enable public network access on the storage account
correctAnswers: Point on-premises DNS (conditional forwarding) at the Azure Private DNS zone for the storage endpoint
explanation: On-premises resolvers must forward the storage FQDN to the Azure Private DNS zone so it resolves to the private endpoint IP.

prompt: Order the steps to give a storage account a private endpoint with no public exposure.
reorderItems: Create a private endpoint targeting the storage account's 'blob' sub-resource
Integrate the endpoint with a Private DNS zone
Verify the storage account's hostname resolves to the private IP from within the VNet
Set Public network access to Disabled
explanation: The endpoint and DNS integration must exist and be verified before it's safe to cut off public access entirely.

prompt: Order the steps to let an App Service reach an on-premises database over a site-to-site VPN while keeping its public inbound endpoint.
reorderItems: Establish a site-to-site VPN connecting the VNet to on-premises
Enable VNet integration on the App Service, pointing to that VNet
Confirm outbound calls from the app reach the on-premises database
Leave the app's default public inbound endpoint unchanged
explanation: The VPN must exist before VNet integration can use it, and verification follows; the public endpoint is simply left as-is throughout.

prompt: Configure this simplified private endpoint screen for a Key Vault that must have zero public exposure.
explanation: Key Vault's private endpoint sub-resource is 'vault'; DNS integration lets clients resolve the private IP; disabling public access removes exposure entirely.

prompt: Proposed solution: Rely on SQL Managed Instance's default deployment into a delegated subnet secured with NSGs, with ExpressRoute private peering to on-premises. Does this solution meet the goal?
choices: Yes
No
correctAnswers: Yes
explanation: SQL MI is never placed on a public multi-tenant network — its delegated-subnet deployment plus ExpressRoute private peering satisfies both reachability and no public exposure.

prompt: Proposed solution: Enable a public endpoint on the SQL Managed Instance and restrict it with a firewall rule allowing only the on-premises public IP. Does this solution meet the goal?
choices: Yes
No
correctAnswers: No
explanation: This still exposes a public endpoint to the internet, even if restricted by IP — it does not meet 'never exposed to the public internet'.

prompt: Proposed solution: Configure a service endpoint for SQL on the on-premises router. Does this solution meet the goal?
choices: Yes
No
correctAnswers: No
explanation: Service endpoints extend a VNet's identity to a service over the Azure backbone — they don't apply to on-premises routers, so this misapplies the concept entirely.

prompt: Which command creates a private endpoint targeting the blob sub-resource of a storage account?
choices: az network private-endpoint create --name PE1 --resource-group RG1 --vnet-name VNet1 --subnet Subnet1 --private-connection-resource-id <storageId> --group-id blob --connection-name conn1
az network private-link create --name PE1 --storage stg1 --type blob
az storage account private-endpoint add --name stg1 --subnet Subnet1
az network vnet subnet update --name Subnet1 --private-endpoint stg1
correctAnswers: az network private-endpoint create --name PE1 --resource-group RG1 --vnet-name VNet1 --subnet Subnet1 --private-connection-resource-id <storageId> --group-id blob --connection-name conn1
explanation: `az network private-endpoint create` with --group-id (the sub-resource, here blob) is the real syntax. The other three subcommands don't exist.

prompt: After creating the private endpoint, which command removes ALL public reachability from the storage account?
choices: az storage account update --name stg1 --resource-group RG1 --public-network-access Disabled
az storage account update --name stg1 --resource-group RG1 --default-action Deny
az storage account network-rule remove --account-name stg1 --ip-address 0.0.0.0/0
az network private-endpoint update --name PE1 --disable-public true
correctAnswers: az storage account update --name stg1 --resource-group RG1 --public-network-access Disabled
explanation: --public-network-access Disabled shuts the public endpoint entirely. --default-action Deny still allows firewall exceptions (selected networks/IPs); the other commands are invented or incomplete.

prompt: Which pair of commands makes the storage account's name resolve to its private IP from within the VNet?
choices: az network private-dns zone create --name privatelink.blob.core.windows.net + az network private-dns link vnet create
az network dns zone create --name blob.core.windows.net + az network dns record-set a add-record
az network private-endpoint dns update --name PE1 --auto
az network vnet update --name VNet1 --dns-server 168.63.129.16
correctAnswers: az network private-dns zone create --name privatelink.blob.core.windows.net + az network private-dns link vnet create
explanation: Private endpoints resolve through the privatelink.* Private DNS zone linked to the VNet. A public DNS zone or changing VNet DNS servers doesn't create the privatelink record mapping.

prompt: Which command enables a SERVICE endpoint for Azure Storage on Subnet1 (the service keeps a restricted public IP)?
choices: az network vnet subnet update --vnet-name VNet1 --name Subnet1 --resource-group RG1 --service-endpoints Microsoft.Storage
az network private-endpoint create --subnet Subnet1 --group-id storage
az storage account update --name stg1 --service-endpoint Subnet1
az network nsg rule create --nsg-name NSG1 --service-tag Storage
correctAnswers: az network vnet subnet update --vnet-name VNet1 --name Subnet1 --resource-group RG1 --service-endpoints Microsoft.Storage
explanation: Service endpoints are enabled on the SUBNET via --service-endpoints. A private endpoint is a different feature (private IP); the other two syntaxes are wrong.

prompt: What distinguishes `--default-action Deny` from `--public-network-access Disabled` on a storage account?
choices: Deny keeps the public endpoint but only allows configured exceptions (IPs/subnets); Disabled shuts the public endpoint entirely
They are strictly equivalent
Disabled only blocks HTTP, Deny blocks HTTPS too
Deny applies to blobs only, Disabled to all services
correctAnswers: Deny keeps the public endpoint but only allows configured exceptions (IPs/subnets); Disabled shuts the public endpoint entirely
explanation: Default-action Deny is the resource firewall's allow-list mode; public-network-access Disabled removes public reachability completely (private endpoint only).
